content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 21, 1999

Armando Gomez
Elections Clerk
Ethics Commission
City and County of San Francisco
1390 Market Street, Suite 801
San Francisco, California 94102-5302

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-99-192

Dear Mr. Gomez:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your request is a general question relating to committee filing requirements, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

What are the filing requirements for a committee that has filed a copy of a Statement of Organization, Form 410, with your office?

FACTS

After filing a Form 410, Statement of Organization with your office, a committee failed to file subsequent campaign statements. The committee did not receive or spend $1,000 or more during 1998.

___________________________

¹Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

²Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (C)(3).)

Our File No. I-99-192
Page 2

During our telephone conversation of July 14, 1999, you indicated the Statement of
Organization was filed by a candidate running in San Francisco's November 3, 1998, election.
You indicated that the candidate has not filed any campaign statements in connection with this
election other than the Form 410, Statement of Organization.

CONCLUSION

Candidates receiving less than $1,000 in contributions and making less than $1,000 in
expenditures in connection with the November 3rd election, were required to file a Form 470 by
October 5, 1998, the filing deadline for the first pre-election statement.

When a filing officer is aware that a candidate has failed to file the required campaign
statements, the filing officer has a duty to notify the candidate. A filing officer is aware of a
candidate's obligation to file disclosure statements when the filing officer has received a Form
410, Statement of Organization, or a Declaration of Candidacy, or any other election document
filed by the candidate.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires candidates and committees to file periodic reports disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made.

Candidates who receive less than $1,000 in contributions and who make less than $1,000
in expenditures during a calendar year may file Form 470. (Section 84206.)

Candidates who receive contributions totaling $1,000 or more must file a Statement of
Organization, Form 410 and file the Form 490, Candidate/Officeholder Campaign Statement-
Long Form in connection with the election. (Sections 84101 and 84200.5.)

Candidates who fail to file disclosure statements should be sent a non-filer notification
letter with instructions to file a Form 470 or a Form 490, whichever is applicable.

In addition, candidates who file a Form 410 and who never qualify as a committee
should be instructed to file a Form 415, Recipient Committee Statement of Termination,
terminating the Statement of Organization, Form 410, with a note to the Secretary of State's
Office explaining that the committee never qualified.

As noted above, notification of non-filers is one of the statutory duties of a filing officer.
(Section 81010 and Regulation 18110.) Failure to file the required statements places the

Our File No. I-99-192
Page 3


candidate and his/her committee in possible violation of the provisions of the Act and subject to
late fines. (Section 91013.)

If you have any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant
